summary: Severe security posture with critical hardcoded credentials exposed over unencrypted HTTP and multiple command injection vulnerabilities in skills allowing arbitrary code execution.

structuredSummary.overview: Severe security posture with critical hardcoded credentials exposed over unencrypted HTTP and multiple command injection vulnerabilities in skills allowing arbitrary code execution.
critical_issues: Hardcoded MiniMax API Key in config file — credential exposed via backups/version control
Model API Key transmitted over HTTP to 35.220.164.252:3888 — actively intercepted in plaintext
Hardcoded Feishu App Secret — enables account takeover if config leaked
Command/argument injection in 15+ skill files — allows arbitrary system command execution
Hardcoded Gateway Auth Token — exposes admin API to anyone with config access
recommendations: 1. Move all API keys to environment variables or secrets manager immediately
2. Change MiniMax and Feishu API keys/secrets NOW — assume compromise
3. Switch provider endpoint from HTTP to HTTPS encrypting traffic
4. Audit and sanitize all skill files with exec/shell patterns — remove or restrict
5. Enable audit logging and configure agent sandboxing with tool restrictions

findings.title: API key for provider "zhangbo" is hardcoded in config
findings.warning: Hardcoded API keys can be leaked through backups, version control, or log files — leading to unauthorized model usage and unexpected billing charges.
findings.evidence: models.providers.zhangbo.apiKey = "sk-mtx*****************************************BR2N" (51 chars)
findings.remediation: Move the key to an environment variable (e.g. OPENCLAW_PROVIDER_KEY) and reference it in config, then rotate the exposed key.

findings.title: Provider "zhangbo" uses unencrypted HTTP
findings.warning: API keys and model prompts are sent in plaintext — any network eavesdropper can intercept credentials and sensitive conversation data.
findings.evidence:
models.providers.zhangbo.baseUrl = "http://35.220.164.252:3888/v1"
API keys and model traffic are transmitted in plaintext over the network.
findings.remediation: Switch to HTTPS endpoint, or tunnel through SSH/VPN if the endpoint does not support TLS.

findings.title: Enabled plugins have no explicit permission restrictions
findings.warning: A compromised or malicious plugin can access all tools and data without any boundary, potentially exfiltrating sensitive information or executing harmful actions.
findings.evidence:
2 plugin(s) enabled: openclaw-plugin-deepsafe, feishu
None define permissions, allowList, or denyList.
findings.remediation: Consider adding per-plugin permission constraints to limit the blast radius of a compromised plugin.

findings.title: No logging or audit trail configured
findings.warning: Without audit logging, you cannot detect, investigate, or prove security incidents — malicious actions will leave no trace.
findings.evidence: No logging, audit, or log configuration section found in openclaw.json.
findings.remediation: Enable logging in openclaw.json to maintain an audit trail of agent actions. Consider setting logging.level to 'info' or higher.

findings.title: No sandbox or tool restriction configured for agents
findings.warning: Without sandboxing, agents can access your entire filesystem, network, and system commands — a single compromised agent could cause full system compromise.
findings.evidence:
No sandbox, isolation, or allowedTools configuration found.
Agents may have unrestricted access to system resources.
findings.remediation: Configure agent sandboxing (filesystem, network, command restrictions) in openclaw.json to limit agent capabilities.

findings.title: Provider "zhangbo" uses a non-standard endpoint
findings.warning: Custom model endpoints may log your prompts and responses — your code, conversations, and sensitive data could be stored on unknown third-party servers.
findings.evidence:
models.providers.zhangbo.baseUrl = "http://35.220.164.252:3888/v1"
This endpoint is not a recognized major provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Azure).
findings.remediation: Verify you trust this endpoint's operator and their data handling policies. Review their privacy/security documentation.

findings.title: Model API Key transmitted over HTTP
findings.warning: The MiniMax API key is sent to http://35.220.164.252:3888 in cleartext, exposing credentials to network interception.
findings.evidence: models.providers.zhangbo.baseUrl = "http://35.220.164.252:3888/v1"
findings.remediation: Change baseUrl to HTTPS (https://35.220.164.252:3888/v1) or use a local model provider with HTTPS.

findings.title: Hardcoded MiniMax API Key
findings.warning: API key is hardcoded in config file. If this file is version-controlled or shared, the key is compromised.
findings.evidence: models.providers.zhangbo.apiKey = "sk-mtxeVbPPoxX5tTY8e67DzE2WlmtOPP4opgrvyv0bibfuBR2N"
findings.remediation: Use environment variable: set apiKey to ${MINIMAX_API_KEY} or load from secure vault.

findings.title: Hardcoded Feishu App Secret
findings.warning: Feishu app secret is hardcoded, allowing account takeover if config is exposed.
findings.evidence: channels.feishu.appSecret = "SQCosy2itXH9MQUMPXe6sdt6HcOWVX7i"
findings.remediation: Load appSecret from environment variable (e.g., ${FEISHU_APP_SECRET}) or secrets manager.

findings.title: Hardcoded Feishu Verification Token
findings.warning: Webhook verification token is hardcoded, enabling attackers to forge events if compromised.
findings.evidence: channels.feishu.verificationToken = "SaFV2nZEajlwNyKw87H0lb73MHasFpbI"
findings.remediation: Move to environment variable or secrets manager.

findings.title: Hardcoded Gateway Auth Token
findings.warning: Gateway authentication token is hardcoded in config, exposing the admin API.
findings.evidence: gateway.auth.token = "c2bb17277256b651b6c9458222e1ee7ae0d313892a71f41d"
findings.remediation: Use environment variable (e.g., ${GATEWAY_TOKEN}) instead of hardcoding.

findings.title: Gateway bind to loopback only
findings.warning: Gateway binds to loopback (127.0.0.1), limiting remote administration but also preventing legitimate remote access if needed.
findings.evidence: gateway.bind = "loopback"
findings.remediation: If remote access is needed, bind to specific IP with strong auth; otherwise this is acceptable.

findings.title: Feishu group policy uses allowlist
findings.warning: Group access is restricted to allowlist (oc_zhangbo), which is good practice.
findings.evidence: channels.feishu.groupPolicy = "allowlist", groupAllowFrom = ["oc_zhangbo"]
findings.remediation: No action needed - this is a secure configuration.

findings.title: Command/argument injection pattern detected
findings.warning: Shell expansion or command chaining in skill content can allow arbitrary code execution when the AI processes tool arguments.
findings.evidence:
Injection patterns found:
  Line 3: description: Manage Apple Notes via the `memo` CLI on macOS (create, view, edit, delete, search, move, and export notes)...
  Line 10: Use `memo notes` to manage Apple Notes directly from the terminal. Create, view, edit, delete, search, move notes betwee...
  Line 13: - Install (Homebrew): `brew tap antoniorodr/memo && brew install antoniorodr/memo/memo`
Patterns like ${...}, `...`, $(...), or pipe-to-shell can inject arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Remove shell expansion syntax. Use explicit, sanitized parameters instead of dynamic command construction.

findings.title: Command/argument injection pattern detected
findings.warning: Shell expansion or command chaining in skill content can allow arbitrary code execution when the AI processes tool arguments.
findings.evidence:
Injection patterns found:
  Line 3: description: Manage Apple Reminders via the `remindctl` CLI on macOS (list, add, edit, complete, delete). Supports lists...
  Line 10: Use `remindctl` to manage Apple Reminders directly from the terminal. It supports list filtering, date-based views, and ...
  Line 13: - Install (Homebrew): `brew install steipete/tap/remindctl`
Patterns like ${...}, `...`, $(...), or pipe-to-shell can inject arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Remove shell expansion syntax. Use explicit, sanitized parameters instead of dynamic command construction.

findings.title: Command/argument injection pattern detected
findings.warning: Shell expansion or command chaining in skill content can allow arbitrary code execution when the AI processes tool arguments.
findings.evidence:
Injection patterns found:
  Line 338: | `cn-zh` | 中国 |
  Line 339: | `us-en` | 美国 |
  Line 340: | `uk-en` | 英国 |
Patterns like ${...}, `...`, $(...), or pipe-to-shell can inject arbitrary commands.
findings.remediation: Remove shell expansion syntax. Use explicit, sanitized parameters instead of dynamic command construction.

findings.title: Skill "duckduckgo-search" grants broad shell execution permissions
findings.warning: Wildcard shell access means the AI can run any command on your system — including destructive operations like rm -rf or data exfiltration.
findings.evidence:
allowed-tools declaration: Bash(duckduckgo-search:*), Bash(python:*), Bash(pip:*), Bash(uv:*)
High-risk entries: bash(python:*)
These allow the skill to execute arbitrary commands via shell.
findings.remediation: Restrict allowed-tools to specific commands instead of wildcards. For example, use Bash(pip:install) instead of Bash(pip:*).

findings.title: Command/argument injection pattern detected

  Line 33: Fast file-name and content search using `fd` and `rg` (ripgrep).

findings.remediation: Remove shell expansion syntax. Use explicit, sanitized parameters instead of dynamic command construction.

  Line 16: - **Env:** `OBSIDIAN_VAULT=/home/ruslan/webdav/data/ruslain`
Patterns like ${...}, `...`, $(...), or pipe-to-shell can inject arbitrary commands.

findings.title: Dangerous execution primitive in skill file
findings.warning: This skill contains code that can execute arbitrary system commands — a compromised or malicious skill could delete files, install malware, or steal credentials.
findings.evidence:
Matched patterns:
  Line 14: import subprocess
  Line 81: result = subprocess.run(
  Line 87: except (FileNotFoundError, subprocess.TimeoutExpired):
These functions can execute arbitrary system commands.
findings.remediation: Validate and sanitize all inputs before passing to execution functions. Consider using a sandbox or allow-list for permitted commands.

findings.title: apple-notes delete operation - interactive but destructive
findings.warning: The memo CLI supports deleting notes (`memo notes -d`) which is a destructive operation, though it requires interactive selection by default.
findings.evidence:
- Delete a note: `memo notes -d`
  - Interactive selection of note to delete.
findings.remediation: This is acceptable as delete requires interactive user confirmation. Consider documenting that automation scripts should use caution when piping input to this command.

findings.title: apple-reminders delete operations - destructive but require IDs
findings.warning: The remindctl CLI supports deleting reminders and lists (`remindctl delete`, `remindctl list --delete`) which are destructive, though they require specific IDs.
findings.evidence:
- Delete by id: `remindctl delete 4A83 --force`
- Delete list: `remindctl list Work --delete`
findings.remediation: Acceptable as operations require explicit IDs. The --force flag is noted which is appropriate for automation scenarios.

findings.title: apple-reminders list delete - destructive account-level operation
findings.warning: Deleting a reminder list (`--delete`) permanently removes all reminders within that list - a batch destructive action.
findings.evidence: - Delete list: `remindctl list Work --delete`
findings.remediation: Consider adding a warning about batch deletion implications or requiring confirmation for list deletion.

findings.title: Plaintext secret found: OpenAI-style API key (sk-...)
findings.warning: Credentials stored in plaintext can be extracted by any process with file access — leading to account takeover, unauthorized API usage, and financial loss.
findings.remediation: Remove the secret from this file, rotate the compromised credential, and use environment variables or a secret manager instead.

findings.title: Personally identifiable information (PII) in session data
findings.warning: Stored PII increases your exposure in a data breach and may violate privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA). Personal data could also be inadvertently sent to third-party model providers.
findings.evidence:
Detected PII types: IP address (potential internal host)
PII stored in session history may violate data protection regulations and increases breach impact.
findings.remediation: Redact or anonymize PII in stored sessions. Consider enabling auto-redaction for future memory writes.

findings.warning: Credentials stored in plaintext can be extracted by any process with file access — leading to account takeover, unauthorized API usage, and financial loss.
findings.remediation: Remove the secret from this file, rotate the compromised credential, and use environment variables or a secret manager instead.

findings.title: Personally identifiable information (PII) in session data
findings.warning: Stored PII increases your exposure in a data breach and may violate privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA). Personal data could also be inadvertently sent to third-party model providers.
